info.name: Ezeebit Wallet API
info.description:
Comprehensive tests for the Ezeebit merchant wallet (hold / convert / withdraw).

How to use:
1. Start the app: `docker compose up -d` then `mvn spring-boot:run` (see README.md). It listens on http://localhost:8080 and seeds merchants 1 and 2.
2. Import this collection and the `ezeebit-local` environment (or rely on the built-in collection variables).
3. Run the folders top-to-bottom (or use the Collection Runner). Requests chain via collection variables (quoteId, withdrawalId, payoutReference, ledgerCursor).

Conventions:
- Seed deposits use fixed Idempotency-Keys so re-running the collection stays idempotent (no runaway balances).
- Happy-path conversions/withdrawals generate a fresh Idempotency-Key per run via a pre-request script, so they are repeatable; the paired 'replay' request reuses that same key to prove idempotency.
- Error-path requests assert the RFC-7807 `code` and HTTP status.

request.description: Deposit funds (Task 1). The fixed Idempotency-Key makes re-runs safe: a replay returns the original result instead of crediting again.
All balances the merchant holds (Task 1).
Audit history for the USDT account, newest first (Task 1). Keyset pagination: the response `nextCursor` is captured for the next page.
Fetch older entries using the cursor from the previous page. If nextCursor was empty, `before=` is ignored and the first page is returned.
First call of an idempotency demo. The next request replays the same key and must return the identical result with no second credit.

item.name: Deposit — idempotent replay (2nd call, same key)

exec: pm.test('same balance as first call (no double credit)', () => {

item.name: Deposit — key conflict (same key, different body) → 422

request.description: Reusing an idempotency key with a different body is rejected — retries must be identical.
Mutating money endpoints require an Idempotency-Key header.
Get a rate quote locked for a short TTL (Task 2). The quoteId is captured for execution.

exec: // Fresh idempotency key each run; the replay request reuses this same value.

request.description: Execute against the exact quoted rate (Task 2). Debits USDT, credits ZAR atomically.
Replaying the same Idempotency-Key returns the original conversion — no double execution.

item.name: Execute conversion — reuse spent quote (new key) → 409

request.description: A quote is single-use even with a different idempotency key: the quote state machine rejects the second execution.
Cannot convert a currency into itself.

exec: pm.test('status PENDING and holds funds', () => pm.expect(w.status).to.eql('PENDING'));
// The payout reference now includes the routed partner code and is assigned at
// submission (async), so capture it from the 'Get withdrawal status' poll instead.

request.description: Request a payout (Task 3). Funds are held immediately; settlement is asynchronous via the payout rail.
The flaky-mobile double-tap: the same Idempotency-Key returns the original withdrawal, holding funds once.
Poll a withdrawal. Within ~1s the stub rail settles it to COMPLETED (default config). Captures payoutReference for the manual callback demo.

USDC routes via the on-chain partner (any country), but merchant 1 holds no USDC, so the hold fails: a merchant can never withdraw money they don't have. (An unroutable currency like NGN/KES for this ZA merchant would instead be NO_PAYOUT_ROUTE, before any hold.)
Merchant 1 is seeded with a 5,000 USDT per-withdrawal cap; 6,000 is rejected before any funds are held.
Stablecoin payouts require a blockchain `address`; fiat requires `accountNumber` + `bankCode`. Malformed destinations are rejected up front.

item.name: Task 4 · Incoming Payments
item.description: Accept an incoming crypto payment through its pending -> available lifecycle. Dedupe by (txHash, outputIndex); credits exactly once at the confirmation threshold (default 3).

request.description: First notification: the payment is PENDING (visible, unspendable).
Pending on-chain money is visible as pendingIncoming, separate from the spendable balance.
At the threshold the payment CONFIRMS: a single INCOMING_CREDIT makes the funds spendable.

exec: pm.test('204 No Content (no double credit)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(204));

request.description: A duplicate notification is a safe no-op — the payment is credited exactly once.
A replay whose immutable facts (here the amount) disagree with the recorded payment is a genuine conflict.
List the merchant's incoming payments and their settle status.

item.name: Task 5 · Auto-Settle
item.description: Merchant 1 is seeded to convert 50% of every incoming USDT to ZAR at market rate. Auto-settle is driven by the outbox shortly after a payment confirms.

request.description: Percentage must be in (0, 100].

item.name: Trigger — confirm a fresh USDT payment

request.description: Confirm 100 USDT; auto-settle should convert 50 USDT -> ZAR shortly after.

exec: const p = err ? null : res.json().find(x => x.txHash === pm.environment.get('settleTx'));

request.description: Polls until the outbox-driven auto-settle marks the payment SETTLED.

item.name: Task 6 · Payout Routing
item.description: Withdrawals are routed to a payout partner by country + currency + limit + health, with priority failover. Seeds: za-swift-eft (ZAR, cap 1000, prio 1), za-payfast (ZAR, prio 2). No KES partner exists.

request.description: A small ZAR payout routes to the priority-1 partner za-swift-eft.

exec: else { pm.test('routed to za-swift-eft', () => pm.expect(w && w.partnerCode).to.eql('za-swift-eft')); }

request.description: Over za-swift-eft's 1000 cap, so routing bypasses it to za-payfast.
Toggling health off demonstrates live failover: subsequent small ZAR payouts route to za-payfast.
There is deliberately no KES partner, so the payout is unroutable and rejected before any funds are held.

item.name: Internal · Payout Rail Callbacks
item.description: The payout rail's settlement webhook. In this build the in-process stub rail drives this automatically ~1s after submission, so a manually-run callback may hit an already-settled withdrawal (409 ILLEGAL_WITHDRAWAL_STATE) — that guard is exactly what makes duplicate callbacks safe. To exercise it yourself, set `wallet.payout.settlement-delay-ms` high in application.yml so the withdrawal stays SUBMITTED, then fire these.

exec: // The payout rail submits asynchronously via the transactional outbox
// (poll interval ~1s). Wait until the withdrawal reaches SUBMITTED and the
// rail reference exists before firing the callback, otherwise it 404s.
pm.sendRequest({ url: base + '/merchants/' + mid + '/withdrawals/' + wid, method: 'GET' }, (err, res) => {
pm.test('204 No Content or 409 (already settled)', () => pm.expect([204, 409]).to.include(pm.response.code));

request.description: Mark the held payout as completed. Idempotent: a duplicate SUCCEEDED callback is a no-op.

exec: pm.test('204 No Content or 409 (already settled)', () => pm.expect([204, 409]).to.include(pm.response.code));

request.description: Mark the payout failed; the held funds are released back to the balance via a compensating ledger entry.
Count of ledger movements (tagged by type/currency). Other metrics: wallet.outbox.processed, wallet.ledger.invariant.violations (must be 0).
